verses.text: These are the countries which the children of Israel inherited in the land of Canaan, which Eleazar the priest, and Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the fathers of the tribes of the children of Israel, distributed for inheritance to them. By lot was their inheritance, as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses, for the nine tribes, and for the half tribe. For Moses had given the inheritance of two tribes and a half tribe on the other side Jordan: but unto the Levites he gave none inheritance among them. The children of Joseph were two tribes, Manasseh and Ephraim: therefore they gave no part unto the Levites in the land, save cities to dwell in, with their suburbs for their cattle and substance. As the LORD commanded Moses, so the children of Israel did, and they divided the land. Guidance: A three-fold leadership signs the deed: priest, general, tribal heads. Not Joshua alone. The lot keeps human favoritism out — God allots, men distribute. The Levites get cities scattered among everyone else; God's ministers will live in 48 towns across all the tribes, so no region of Israel is without a priest within walking distance.

Then the children of Judah came unto Joshua in Gilgal: and Caleb the son of Jephunneh the Kenezite said unto him, Thou knowest the thing that the LORD said unto Moses the man of God concerning me and thee in Kadesh-barnea. Guidance: Forty-five years later, Caleb walks up to Joshua with a memory and a claim. The Kenezite — a Gentile-rooted family attached to Judah. Caleb, the only other man besides Joshua who walked out of Egypt and is still alive. His opening line refuses small talk: 'Thou knowest the thing.' Two old soldiers do not need a long preamble.

Forty years old was I when Moses the servant of the LORD sent me from Kadesh-barnea to espy out the land; and I brought him word again as it was in mine heart. Nevertheless my brethren that went up with me made the heart of the people melt: but I wholly followed the LORD my God. Moses sware on that day, saying, Surely the land whereon thy feet have trodden shall be thine inheritance, and thy children's for ever, because thou hast wholly followed the LORD my God. Guidance: Three times in eight verses Caleb says the phrase 'wholly followed the LORD.' It is his life's verdict. He gives the same number of words to his own faithfulness as to the others' fear. He is not boasting; he is reporting the LORD's own assessment of him (Num 14:24). When God speaks well of you, repeat it to remember it.

Now, behold, the LORD hath kept me alive, as he said, these forty and five years, even since the LORD spake this word unto Moses, while the children of Israel wandered in the wilderness: and now, lo, I am this day fourscore and five years old. As yet I am as strong this day as I was in the day that Moses sent me: as my strength was then, even so is my strength now, for war, both to go out, and to come in. Guidance: He counts the years like a man who has been waiting. Forty-five years from the spy mission. He calls his survival the LORD's keeping, not his own constitution. At 85, his line is not 'I have served enough.' It is 'as my strength was then, even so is my strength now.' Some saints retire when God still has assignments.

Now, behold, the LORD hath kept me alive, as he said, these forty and five years, even since the LORD spake this word unto Moses, while the children of Israel wandered in the wilderness: and now, lo, I am this day fourscore and five years old. As yet I am as strong this day as I was in the day that Moses sent me: as my strength was then, even so is my strength now, for war, both to go out, and to come in. Guidance: He counts the years like a man who has been waiting. Forty-five years from the spy mission. He calls his survival the LORD's keeping, not his own constitution. At 85, his line is not 'I have served enough.' It is 'as my strength was then, even so is my strength now.' Some saints retire when God still has assignments.

Now therefore give me this mountain, whereof the LORD spake in that day; for thou heardest in that day how the Anakims were there, and the cities were great and fenced: if so be the LORD will be with me, then I shall be able to drive them out, as the LORD said. Guidance: The most famous line of an old man's faith. Other tribes might have prayed for an easier portion in their dotage; Caleb asks for the giants' mountain. The Anakim — the same giants whose name made his fellow spies turn back. He wants the harder assignment because the LORD said it. 'If so be the LORD will be with me' — humble, not presumptuous. He does not claim his strength; he claims his God. Faith aged eighty-five still asks for the giants.

Joshua blessed him, and gave unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh Hebron for an inheritance. Hebron therefore became the inheritance of Caleb the son of Jephunneh the Kenezite unto this day, because that he wholly followed the LORD God of Israel. Guidance: Joshua does not negotiate. He blesses. The two who came out of Egypt together honor each other in their final assignment. Hebron — the city of Abraham's tomb, the burial cave of Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, Leah — is given to the man whose faith most resembles theirs. The narrator pronounces the verdict for the fourth time: 'because he wholly followed the LORD.'

Joshua blessed him, and gave unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh Hebron for an inheritance. Hebron therefore became the inheritance of Caleb the son of Jephunneh the Kenezite unto this day, because that he wholly followed the LORD God of Israel. Guidance: Joshua does not negotiate. He blesses. The two who came out of Egypt together honor each other in their final assignment. Hebron — the city of Abraham's tomb, the burial cave of Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, Leah — is given to the man whose faith most resembles theirs. The narrator pronounces the verdict for the fourth time: 'because he wholly followed the LORD.'

The name of Hebron before was Kirjath-arba; Arba was a great man among the Anakims. And the land had rest from war. Guidance: Hebron used to be named after the biggest giant in town — 'City of Arba.' Now it is the inheritance of an 85-year-old shepherd. The footnote is the whole point: God reassigns geography in favor of faith. The chapter closes the way Joshua 11:23 did — 'the land had rest from war.' Caleb's request happens inside that rest. Even in seasons of peace, the personally promised mountain may still need taking.
